channel.title: Texts on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Texts on emsenn.net

item.title: Anarcho-Nihilism Cannot Produce Fascism
item.description:
¶Abstract

Fascism is a determinate phenomenon with necessary conditions. It requires palingenetic ultranationalism, mass mobilization through identifiable stages, an exceptional state form fusing party and sovereignty, corporatist reorganization of capital, biopolitical population management, and the sacralization of political life into civic religion. These conditions are not optional features; they are the structural minimum. A formation that lacks any of them is not fascism.

Anarcho-nihilism cannot satisfy any of them. Not one. Its refusal of teleology dissolves the rebirth myth. Its refusal of mass unity prevents movement formation. Its refusal of sovereignty prevents state capture. Its refusal of identity hierarchy prevents biopolitical sorting. Its refusal of sacrality prevents political religion. These are not contingent absences — they are structural consequences of anarcho-nihilism’s core commitments. The framework does not merely fail to produce fascism; it actively dissolves the metaphysical supports fascism requires.

item.title: Relational Adaptation as a System
item.description:
¶Assumed audience

Someone who has understood why universalist coordination breaks under stress and wants to know what the alternative looks like — not as a theory but as a set of interlocking practices.

¶What this is not

This is not a program. There is no step-by-step implementation guide because the whole point is that programs — plans designed in advance, generalized across contexts, ratified through universal procedures — are what collapse destroys. What follows is a description of six structural features that, when they operate together, produce a framework with high adaptive capacity. They are not steps. They are properties of a system.

item.title: Adaptation Does Not Require a Future
item.description:
¶Abstract

Mutualism’s five structural commitments — cooperative reciprocity, symmetry, universalizability, proceduralism, and epistemic universalism — become operational liabilities under ecological collapse. Each commitment presupposes conditions that collapse destroys: enough time for deliberation, enough capacity for universal inclusion, enough safety for transparency, enough shared ground for consensus. When those conditions vanish, mutualist communities face a specific failure mode: their procedures for avoiding domination prevent them from acting fast enough to survive.
